coredata.dc:title: Green fluorescent carbon dots functionalized MoO3 nanoparticles for sensing of hypochlorite
coredata.dc:creator: Kateshiya, Mehul R.
Malek, Naved I.
Kumar Kailasa, Suresh
coredata.dc:description: In this study, three different colored blue (B-), green (G-) and yellow (Y-) fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using natural precursor Annona squamosa fruit via acid oxidation method. The as-synthesized B-, G- and Y-CDs displayed intense emission peaks at 445, 514 and 558 nm upon the excitations at 360, 420 and 440 nm, respectively. The functionalization of molybdenum oxide nanoparticles (MoO3 NPs) with three color emissive CDs allows well dispersability in aqueous media and converts the non-fluorescent nature of MoO3 NPs to blue, green and yellow color emissions, which is beneficial for the sensitive assay of hypochlorite (ClO-). It was found that the emission intensity of G-CDs functionalized MoO3 NPs at 516 nm was remarkably quenched by ClO-, whereas ClO- showed least quenching ability towards blue- and yellow- MoO3 NPs. Based on these observations, MoO3 NPs-G-CDs showed a linear fluorescence quenching at 516 nm in the range of 0.010–200 µM with detection limit of 6.8 nM for ClO-. The developed optical sensor affords good selectivity for the detection of ClO- in water samples with good recoveries, which demonstrates great promise of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs in assaying ClO- in complex samples at minimal volume of samples.

HR-TEM images of (a) pure G-CDs, (b) pure MoO3 NPs, (c) MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and (d) MoO3 NPs-G-CDs with ClO- (Insets: Histograms of corresponding images).
XPS data of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs NPs of (a) full scan (b) Mo 3d, (c) N 1s and (d) C 1s.

Fluorescence emission spectra after addition of various oxidizing and free radical agents into (a) B-CDs, (b) Y-CDs and (c) MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and Inset: image of G-CDs-MoO3 NPs (1) ClO-, (2) H2O2, (3) KO2, (4) (NH4)2S2O8, (5) pyrophosphatase, (6) N-Bromosuccinimide, (7) KnMO4, (8) K2Cr2O7 and (9) Na2HPO4 under UV light.
Fluorescence emission spectra of B-, Y- and G- CDs and MoO3 NPs-G-CDs without and with addition of ClO- (200 µM) and (b) photographic image of corresponding solutions under UV-light.

(a) Fluorescence responses of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs upon addition of ClO- (0.010–200 µM). Inset: Photographic image of fluorescence changes of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs with different concentrations of ClO- (0.010–200 µM) under UV lamp at 365 nm and (b) calibration graph plotted between the raito (I0/I) at 516 nm and ClO- concentration in the range of 0.01–1 µM.
Schematic representation for the fabrication of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs for the detection of ClO-.
Analytical performance of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs-based fluorescence method for sensing of ClO- with reported methods in the literature.
Assaying of ClO- by using MoO3 NPs-G-CDs as a probe.

head.ce:title: Green fluorescent carbon dots functionalized MoO3 nanoparticles for sensing of hypochlorite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat-395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallbhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallbhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat-395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, three different colored blue (B-), green (G-) and yellow (Y-) fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using natural precursor Annona squamosa fruit via acid oxidation method. The as-synthesized B-, G- and Y-CDs displayed intense emission peaks at 445, 514 and 558 nm upon the excitations at 360, 420 and 440 nm, respectively. The functionalization of molybdenum oxide nanoparticles (MoO3 NPs) with three color emissive CDs allows well dispersability in aqueous media and converts the non-fluorescent nature of MoO3 NPs to blue, green and yellow color emissions, which is beneficial for the sensitive assay of hypochlorite (ClO-). It was found that the emission intensity of G-CDs functionalized MoO3 NPs at 516 nm was remarkably quenched by ClO-, whereas ClO- showed least quenching ability towards blue- and yellow- MoO3 NPs. Based on these observations, MoO3 NPs-G-CDs showed a linear fluorescence quenching at 516 nm in the range of 0.010–200 µM with detection limit of 6.8 nM for ClO-. The developed optical sensor affords good selectivity for the detection of ClO- in water samples with good recoveries, which demonstrates great promise of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs in assaying ClO- in complex samples at minimal volume of samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Molybdenum trioxide (MoO3) exhibits various polymorphs (α-MoO3, β-MoO3 and h-MoO3) [1–4]. It is a n-type semiconductor, which exhibits a wider band gap (2.50–3.2 eV) [5–6]. It has been recognized as a promising material for various applications due to its unique properties (thermochromic, electrochromic and photochromic). Further, the structure of MoO3 with oxygen vacancies allows to tune their electronic structures and optoelectronic properties [7,8]. MoO3 NPs acted as promising materials for a wide range of applications such as supercapacitors [9], biosensing [10], gas sensor [11], electrochemical sensor [12] and water splitting [13]. So far, different types of MoO3 nanostructures such as nanowire [14], nanoparticles [12] nanoribbon [15], nanobelts [16], nanorod [17], nanoflakes [11], nanosheets [18] and nanofiber [19] have been synthesized and studied their promising applications in various branches of science and technology. Among these materials, MoO3 NPs have emerged as promising materials for various applications because of their features such as high stability, simple synthetic strategies and rapid redox process. Various synthetic routes have been described for the synthesis of MoO3 such as chemical vapor deposition [20], sol–gel [21], hydrothermal [22], electrochemical deposition [23], exfoliation [24], sonochemical [25] and precipitation process [26]. To make them as an attractive materials, the surfaces of MoO3 NPs have been tuned with different nanomaterials and organic molecules such as RuO2 NPs, [27], Au NPs [28], Pt NPs [29], α-Fe2O3 [30], graphene [31], reduced graphene oxide [32] and ascorbic acid [33]. Moreover, the surfaces of MoO3 NPs were decorated with CDs for their applications in Li-ion battery [34]. Although surface modified MoO3 NPs possess remarkable properties and great promise applications, it still requires the effective surface modifications to produce MoO3 NPs with strong fluorescence emission, which promotes their use in analytical chemistry.

Recent years, the CDs have been recognized as a new class of light emissive nanomaterials, which were accidentally discovered during single-walled carbon nanotube solution purification [35]. CDs are also called as a quasi-zero-dimentional carbon nanostructures materials with a size of < 10 nm, and fluorescence property [36]. Imporatnly, CDs have been considered as ecofriendly nanomaterials, and gaining more popularity in various desciplines including biomedicine, materials and analytical chemistry due to their advantageous properties including biocompatibility, tunable emission, good photostability, chemical inertness, high surface passivation, and high quantum yield (QY) [37]. Several papers have been illustrated the characterization of carbon nanostructures-based particulate maters and their impact on the human health and ecology [38–41]. Noticeably, Annona squamosa is well recognised as edible tropical fruitsand mostly found in Asia and North America, showing some therapeutic properties [42]. Recently, multicolor fluorescent CDs have been successfully fabricated by using several fruits and platn materials as raw materials [43–46]. These reports are inspired us to fabricate CDs with three different emission properties by using Annona squamosa fruit as a precursor. Further, the functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs may allow to exhibit fascinating features in analytical chemistry as compared to unmodified MoO3 NPs, offering a new simple analytical approach for sensing applications.

Hypochlorite (ClO-) solution is a powerful oxidizer that typically used as a disinfectant or bleaching agent for various applications. It is one of the reactive oxygen species, which exhibits promising roles in various biochemical pathways. Importantly, high or abnormal levels of ClO- led to damage several tissues, thereby causing several diorders such as degeneration of neuron, atherosclerosis, cancer, heart disease and arthritis, and cancer [47,48]. It (3–6% solution of ClO-) has been used as a strong oxidizer for various applications including deodorization, water disinfection, deodorization, and bleaching [49]. ClO- is an active component in everyday sanitising sprays; nevertheless, owing to the corrosive nature of ClO-s, if ClO- comes into direct contact with a human body part, it can cause eye burning, skin and organ damage [50]. It also plays a critical role in varous physiological pathway in living systesm including cell differentiation, innate immunity, and migration [51]. In view of this, the development of analytical strategy is very important for monitoring of trace level ClO- in water and living systems [52]. As a result, much effort has been dedicated to develop varoiu analytical approaches including colorimetry [53], chromatography [54], voltammetry [55], potentiometry [56] and CdSe-ZnS QDs-based fluorescence spectrometry [57] for trace level identification of ClO-. Among these techniques, fluorescence and colorimetric tehcniqeus were widely used for the detection of ClO-. Briefly, Yan et. al. synthesized fluorescent CdSe-ZnS QDs for sensing of ClO- [57]. The fluorescence intensity of CdSe-ZnS QDs was quenched after the addition of ClO-. Walekar et al. developed a colorimetric sensor by using cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB) decorated Ag NPs (CPB-AgNPs) as a visual reader for the detection of ClO- [64]. The color change of AgNPs yellowish brown to colorless was observed after the addition of ClO- into AgNPs because of aggregation of AgNPs. Using these analytical approaches, ClO- was successfully detected with impressive analytical merits (sensitivity and simplicity). However, these techniques show disadvantages such as time-consuming, poor solubility and toxicity of probe. Further, the integration of functionalized nanostructure materials with fluorescence spectrometry has proven to be an attractive analytical strategy in terms of simplicity, sensitivity and selectivity [38]. To explore the analytical applications of MoO3 NPs, G-CDs were functionalized onto the surface of MoO3 NPs that expoit them as promising and alternative fluorescent probes for sensing of ClO- in water samples.

In this study, first time we introduced a hybrid fluorescent nanosensor by functionalizing the surfaces of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs (MoO3 NPs-G-CDs), offering to develop a simple method for assaying of ClO- in water samples. The non-fluorescent nature of MoO3 NPs was converted into green fluorescent MoO3 NPs via the surface modification with G-CDs. Multi-colored (B-,G-and Y)-CDs were synthesized by acid oxidation method using natural precursor ripe fruit of Annona squamosa as a precusor. It is observed that G-CDs encapsulated MoO3 NPs exhibited high ability to interact with ClO-, thereby resulting a remarkable quenching of emission intensity at 516 nm (Scheme 1 ). The as-fabricated MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were exploited as a simple fluorescent nanosensor for the detection ClO- in water samples, exhibiting a good linearity in the rang of 0.010–200 µM with the detection limit of 6.8 nM. Taking advantage of unique properties of hybrid nanostructures, MoO3 NPs-G-CDs-based fluorescence approach was successfully applied to assay ClO- in water samples (tap, river and canal water).

Ripe Annona squamosa (Sitaphal) fruit was collected from the local market, Surat, Gujarat, India. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. NaOCl, H2SO4 and H3PO4 were purchased from Finar Pvt, Ltd, India. MoO3 powder was obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India

High resolution - transmission electron micrograph (TEM) images were obtained by using HR-TEM instrument (A JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) and Agilent 5500 AFM/SPM system (Agilent, USA). Surface modifications and elemental composition of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were characterized by various spectroscopic techniques (UV–Visisble spectroscopy (Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer, Ocean Optics, USA), fluorescence spectroscopy (Agilent Technologies, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Bruker Alpha-II FTIR spectrometer, Bruker, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific)).

The B-,G- and Y-CDs were successfully prepared by adapting the previously reported procedure [58]. Briefly, Annona squamosa fruits were washed carefully, seeds were removed and passed through the blender to convert into slurry. This slurry was stored in a deep freezer for 2 days at –23 °C. For the synthesis of B-CDs, frozen slurry of (500 mg) Annona squamosa fruit was taken into a 5.0 mL of 34.0 N H2SO4 in a 50 mL reaction flask and sonicated for 4 min and refluxed at 100 °C for 50 min. The pH of the crude product was neutralized with help of NaOH (2.0 M). The final neutralized solution of B-CDs was dialyzed for 24 h in water. For G-CDs synthesis, 500 mg of frozen state Annona squamosa fruit was taken into a 5 mL of water and 10 mL of (40.0 N) H3PO4 was added. The solution mixture was refluxed at 80 °C for 35 to 45 min. Afterwards, pH of the solution was neutralized with 2.0 M of NaOH solution. The product obtained by the neutralized solution of G-CDs was dialyzed for 24 h in water. Likewise, Y-CDs were synthesized by taking frozen state 500 mg of Annona squamosa fruit, dissolving into (40.0 N, 10 mL) H3PO4 solution and refluxing the reaction mixture at 80 °C for 15–20 min. Thereafter, pH was neutralized with 2.0 M NaOH. The final neutralized product of Y-CDs was dialyzed for 24 h in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs was achieved by the following procedure. Firstly, MoO3 NPs was synthesized, briefly MoO3 powder (100 mg, 10 mM) was taken into a 50 mL round bottom flask containing H2O2 (30% v/v, 10 mL) and then the solution was sonicated for 15 min. The above solution was refluxed for 2.5 h at 105 °C, yielding a yellow color solution, which confirms the development of MoO3 NPs with more hydroxy groups. The formed non-fluorescent MoO3 NPs were dried and utilised for the functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs. Briefly, 5.0 mg of as-synthesized MoO3 NPs were dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water and added to the 25 mL reaction flask containing 10 mL of G-CDs solution with constant stirring and allowed the reaction at room temperature for 3 h. After the completion of reaction, the functionalized MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were stored at 4 °C for more use.

In order to explore the utility of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs as fluorescent nanosensor, the sensing application of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was investigated for the detection of ClO-. In a typical procedure, 0.5 mL of reactive oxygen species and oxidizying chemical species (ClO-, H2O2, KO2 , (NH4)2S2O8, pyrophosphatase, N-bromosuccinimide, KnMO4, K2Cr2O7 and Na2HPO4, 500 µM) was introduced separately into 1.0 mL of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and the resultant solution was vortexed for 1 20 s. The sensing ability of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs toward ClO- was investigated by examining the emission spectra of the above solutions (λem/ λex = 516/420 nm). It was noticed that ClO- was completely quenched the emission intensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs, which confirms the sensing ability of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs towards ClO-. To construct calibration graph, the fluorescence spectra were measured by mixing the different concentrations of ClO- (0.010–200 μM, 0.5 mL) separately with MoO3 NPs-G-CDs (1.0 mL). The quantitative assay of ClO- was performed by constructing calibration plot between the emission peak inetensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs in the absence of ClO- (I0) and in the presence of ClO- (I) and concentration of ClO-.

To explore the feasibility of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs as a nanosensor, river water (Tapi, Surat), canal and tap waters were collected and the fluorescence intesnities of collected water samples were performed by spiking ClO- concentration (10, 15 and 20 nM) into the above water samples. The amount of ClO- present in the above water samples were measured based on the calibration graph. The recoveries (%) were estimated by comparing the measured concentration and standard concentration added into water samples.

The ripe of Annona squamosa contains different phytochemicals such as mostly containing carbohydrates, terpenoids and large amounts of different carbohydrates [59]. These compounds play a crucial in the synthesis of CDs with different surface functionalities. The UV–visible absorption spectra of B-, G-, and Y- CDs produced from Annona squamosa fruits are shown in Fig. 1 a-c. The absorption maxima of B-, G-, and Y- CDs were noticed at 306, 316, and (310 and 487) nm, respectively and when exposed to 365 nm wavelength of UV light the CDs displayed (blue, green, and yellow) fluorescent color (Insets of Fig. 1a-c). The π-π* and n- π* electronic transition of CDs were noticed in the absorption spectra, confirming the presence of surface active organic groups such as (-C = C, -C = O, -C = N, and -C-O) on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, when excited three colored B-, G- and Y- CDs at 360, 420 and 440 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths are noticed at 436, 516 and 552 nm for B-, G- and Y- CDs, respectively (Fig. 1a-c).

The fluorescence emission spectra of B-, G- and Y- CDs are investigated by exciting them at different excitation wavelengths (B-CDs − 320–400 nm, G-CDs − 340–460 nm and Y-CDs − 360–480 nm) (Figure S1a-c in Supporting Information) and excitation dependent emission features are demonstrated by the three CDs. While the excitation wavelength increased, the emission maxima of B-, G- and Y- CDs were red shifted and the highest emission intensity at 436, 516 and 552 nm was observed when excited at 360 nm (B-CDs), 420 nm (G-CDs) and 440 (Y-CDs), respectively. These results revealed that the distinct emission characteristics may be due to the existence of different functionality in the CDs surface and small differences in CDs size. The QYs of fabricated three CDs were calculated as 12.5% (B-CDs), G-CDs (11.65%) and Y-CDs (4.60%), respectively, showing higher QY than that of the reported methods [58,60,61].

Furthermore, MoO3 NPs were successfully functionalized with G-CDs for achieving better surface properties and enhancing the selectivity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs toward ClO-. The UV–visible spectra of pure G-CDs, MoO3 NPs and functionalized MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were depicted in Supporting Information of Figure S2a. It can be observed that the pure G-CDs, MoO3 NPs and functionalized MoO3 NPs-G-CDs exhibited absorption maxima at 306, 330 and 325 nm, illustrating the absorbance of MoO3 NPs was slightly decreased by the functionalization with G-CDs. In order to provide effective G-CDs assembly on the surfaces of MoO3 NPs, the emission spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were investigated by using different concentration of MoO3 NPs (5.0 – 20 mg) (Figure S2b in Supporting Information). The emission spectra showed maximum intensity by using 5 mg of MoO3 NPs, demonstrating no change was observed on the fluorescence emission spectra as compared to other concentrations. Thus, 5 mg of MoO3 NPs was selected as optimum concentration for their suface modificaitons with G-CDs, which could facilitate to generate fluorescent properties. These results indicated that G-CDs were successfully functionalized on the surface of MoO3 NPs, allowing to convert non-fluorescent to fluorescent nature of MoO3 NPs. The stability of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was evaluated by monitoring the emission peak of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs at regular intervals (Figure S2c in Supporting Information), revealing the they are stable up to 60 days. The stability study indicated that the MoO3 NPs-G-CDs exhibited good stability, which could be favor to use as a good probe for the sensing application.

The FTIR spectra were studied for the confirmation of surface functional groups on the surface of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs (Figure S3 in Supporting Information). The bands at 581, 867, 988 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of δMo-O-Mo, νMo-O-Mo (vibrations of Mo6+) and νMo = O (Figure S3a in Supporting Information). The band at 853 cm−1 is belonged to the stretching of Mo2–O (doubly connected bridge-oxygen) and the band at 1387 cm−1 is corresponded to the vibrational mode of νMo –OH, which is due to the interaction of Mo with H2O (Figure S3b in Supporting Information). FTIR spectrum of G-CDs displayed the bands at 3432, 2928 and 1640 cm−1 are due to stretchings of − OH, -C-H, and (–C = O and –C = N), respectively (Figure S3c in Supporting Information). The peaks at 1397 and 1233 cm−1 correspond to –C-N and C-O stretchings and at 1057 cm−1 belongs to –O-H bending. However, the characteristics bands in FTIR spectra of pure G-CDs and MoO3 NPs were either slightly changed or disappeared due to the functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs (Figure S3d in Supporting Information).

HR-TEM images are presented in Fig. 2 to further validate the size and morphology of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs. Fig. 2a represents the HR-TEM images of pure G-CDs at 10.0 nm scale bar, revealing the as-synthesized G-CDs are well dispersed with a mean size of 2.41 ± 0.73 nm (Inset of Fig. 2a). The HR-TEM image of pure MoO3 NPs was shown in Fig. 2b, demonstrating the as-synthesized MoO3 NPs were well dispersed with an average particles size of 5.13 ± 1.02 nm (Inset of Fig. 2b). After the functionalization, the size of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was very slightly increased, and well dispersed with a mean size of 5.42 ± 0.85 nm (Inset Fig. 2c).

To confirm the elemental components of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs, XPS spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were studied and shown in Fig. 3 . The full scan spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs represent the presence of Mo N, C, and O elements in MoO3 NPs-G-CDs (Fig. 3a). High-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs showed the binding energies of 236.18 and 233.05 eV, indicating existence of Mo as Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, which confirms Mo4+ and Mo6+ oxidation states of Mo in MoO3 NPs-G-CDs (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s, peak at 399.88 eV indicating the single oxidation state present in MoO3 NPs-G-CDs. Fig. 3d shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, illustrating the carbon (233.05 and 236.18 eV) is in two types of oxidation state in MoO3 NPs-G-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective response of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs towards ClO-
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate surface modification of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs as a fluorescent probe, the sensing ability of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs, pure B-, G- and Y- CDs was studied toward various oxidizing and free radical generating agents (ClO-, H2O2, KO2 , (NH4)2S2O8, pyrophosphatase, N-bromosuccinimide, KnMO4, K2Cr2O7 and Na2HPO4, 500 µM) and the spectra were shown in Fig. 4 . Interestingly, the emission spectra of MoO3 NPs-B- and Y- CDs did not show any significant change in presence of ClO- (Fig. 4a-b), only the fluorescence emission intensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was completely quenched in the presence of ClO- (Fig. 4c). Also, the various oxidizing and free radical agents did not induce any changes in the fluorescence emission intensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs. In order to define the functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs, the emission spectra of three colored CDs (blue, green and yellow) and MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were studied in the presence of ClO- (200 µM) (Fig. 5 a). It can be observed that negligible fluorescence quenching was noticed in the emission spectra of three colored CDs, however, the emission spectrum of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was completely quenched with the addition of ClO- (200 µM), suggesting the functionalization of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs has greatly impacted to act as fluorescent probe for sensing of ClO-. These results suggested the possible application of MoO3 NPs- G-CDs as a probe for quantitave assay of ClO-.

To improve the analytical performance of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs for ClO- sensing, critical factor such as pH effect was investigated for the sensing of ClO- using MoO3 NPs-G-CDs as a probe. The effect of the pH was examined with the help of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (2–12 pH). Figure S4 in Supporting Information shows the fluorescence spectra of MoO3 NPs- G-CDs with and without ClO- at PBS (pH 2–12). At different PBS pH (2.0 – 12.0), the emission intensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs did not change without the addition of ClO-, which indicates the PBS buffer pH did not influence the emission spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs. However, ClO- has shown great tendency to quench the fluorescence intensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs at PBS pH 6.0 as compared to other PBS pHs. Therefore, the quantitative assay of ClO- was carried out by using MoO3 NPs-G-CDs as a sensor at PBS pH = 6.

The sensing mechanism is proposed based on the absorbance, fluorescence lifetime, FTIR spectra and HR-TEM data. The UV–visible spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs with and without ClO- were depicted in Figure S2a of Supporting Information. The characteristic peak at 325 nm was decreased and blue-shifted to 306 nm upon the introduction of ClO-. This spectral changes revealed that the detachment of G-CDs on the surfaces of MoO3 NPs. In addition to this, FT-IR spectra of MoO3 powder, MoO3 NPs, pure G-CDs, MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and MoO3 NPs-G-CDs with ClO- were investigated (Figure S3a-e in Supporting Information). It can be seen that the disappeared bands of δMo-O-Mo, νMo-O-Mo (vibrations of Mo6+) and νMo = O at 581, 867, 988 cm−1 were restored by the addition of ClO-, confirming the breaking of bonds between G-CDs and MoO3 NPs. Further, the mean size of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs-ClO- (5.42 ± 0.85 nm) is very closed to the mean size of unmodified MoO3 NPs, revealing the detachment of G-CDs from the surface of MoO3 NPs (Fig. 2d).

Additionally, the Stern–Volmer equation (I0/I = 1 + Ksv[Q]) was used for possible explanation of sensing mechanism. The calibration graph was plotted by using I0/I and concentration of ClO- [Q], showing a good linearity between I0/I and the concentration of ClO- (0.01 – 0.9 µM) (Fig. 6 b). The quenching constant (Ksv) was found at 3.75 × 10-5 M by using the Stern–Volmer equation, a higher value of (Ksv) was indicated the strong interaction between MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and ClO-. Furthermore, the lifetime spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs with and without ClO- was studied (Figure S5 in Supporting Information). The average lifetimes of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and MoO3 NPs-G-CDs-ClO- were found at 4.68 and 4.63 ns, confirming that there was no change in the lifetime of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs after the addition of ClO-, which supported the static quenching mechanism. To conclude these result, the fluorescence quenching of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was induced by ClO– via energy transfer mechanism. The surface functional groups (–NH2, –OH, –COOH) on MoO3 NPs-G-CDs are key binding sites to interact with ClO- via H-bonding (between –N-H group of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and ClO-) [62], which leads to quench the fluorescence of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs. Moreover, ClO- is high ability to adsorb on the surfaces of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs via H-bonding, resulting to reduce interparticle distance between MoO3 NPs-G-CDs, which leads to the fluorescenc quenching. Thus, there is possibility to transfer the energy from MoO3 NPs-G-CDs to ClO-, revealing the surface modification of MoO3 NPs with G-CDs plays key role for sensing of ClO- via energy transfer mechanism.

Under the optimal conditions, the linear response of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was investigated with respect to ClO- concentration in the range of 0.010 – 200 µM (Fig. 6a). The emission intensity of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs at 516 nm was gradually quenched with increasing concentration of ClO-. As a result, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (I0/I) showed a linear relationship (I0/I = 0.5841[ClO–] + 2.1354) with ClO- concentration from 0.010 to 200 µM (R2 = 0.9904) (Fig. 6b). According to the calibration graph, the limit of detection (LOD) (3σ/S) was estimated as 6.8 nM for ClO-, which indicated that the LOD is either comparable or lower than the reported methods in the literature [54,57,63–76] (Table 1 ). These results indicated that the as-synthesized hybrid MoO3 NPs-G-CDs acted as a sensitive probe for assaying of ClO-.

Before implementing the method for assaying of ClO– in reals samples, the selectivity of the developed probe is also a key parameter. Effects of various inorganic species (H2O2, CH3COO–, HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, SO4 2- Cl-, Br-, I-, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Ni2+ 500 µM) on the influence of emission spectra of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs were studied and these results demonstrated that ClO- has remarkably quenched the fluorescence intensity MoO3 NPs-G-CDs whereas other chemical species did not induce any fluorescence quenching, while the coaddition of ClO- causes complete quenching of emission intensity (Figure S6 in Supporting Information). The selective repsonce of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs toward ClO– is highily suitable for the quantative analysis ClO- in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ClO- in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the potentialty and accuracy of the developed hybrid fluorescent probe, the quantitative assay of ClO- was carried by spiking different concentration of ClO- (10, 15 and 20 nM) and the amount of ClO- was estimated by the standard addition method (Table 2 ). The developed analytical strategy exhibited percentage recoveires in the range of 96.23–99.27% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values < 1.0 (n = 3), demonstrating hybrid MoO3 NPs-G-CDs could be used as a potential fluorescent probe for the detection of ClO- in real samples.

In this method, multi-colored (blue, green and yellow) -CDs were synthesized by the acid oxidation method using Annona squamosa fruit as a precursor. The surface of MoO3 NPs was modified with G-CDs, resulting to fabricated a novel hybrid fluorescent MoO3 NPs-G-CDs nanostructure material. The as-fabricated hydrid MoO3 NPs-G-CDs exhibited green fluorescence at 516 nm. Among three hybrid nanomaterials (B-, G- and Y- CDs decorated MoO3 NPs), green emitting hybrid MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was very sensitive to ClO– and the fluorescence emission of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs was completely quenched by ClO- via the formation H-bonding (functional group (–NH2) of MoO3 NPs-G-CDs and ClO-). Remarkably, the developed hybrid fluorescent sensor displayed several analytical merits including a wider linear range (0.010– 200 μM), lower LODs (6.8 nM), rapid response and selectivity. Moreover, the analytical significance of hybrid MoO3 NPs-G-CDs fluorescent sensor was demonstrated for quantitative detection of ClO- in real samples (river, canal and tap water), implying the as-fabricated hybrid MoO3 NPs-G-CDs could be used as a rapid fluorescent sensor for the analysis of ClO- in water samples and biological systems.
